item.title: Oblivious SpaceSaving: Heavy-Hitter Detection over Fully Homomorphic Encryption
item.description:
Heavy-hitter detection is a core primitive in stream analytics, but running it over sensitive streams on untrusted infrastructure raises important privacy concerns. This work presents Oblivious SpaceSaving, a privacy-preserving reformulation of the classical Space-Saving algorithm for execution over fully homomorphic encryption (FHE).

Core Ideas

  • Encrypted heavy-hitter detection: The system performs exact heavy-hitter computation on encrypted streams without relying on multi-server trust assumptions.
  • Moving Floor abstraction: The main algorithmic idea replaces repeated encrypted magnitude comparisons with equality-based selection against a tracked encrypted floor.
  • Streaming systems design: The paper combines this idea with parallel victim selection and a hierarchical asynchronous ingestion pipeline for end-to-end encrypted processing.

Results

  • Reduces encrypted update cost by up to 2.74x over a naive oblivious baseline.
  • Sustains encrypted ingestion throughput of up to 4.30 items/s with sub-second amortized latency.
  • Preserves the deterministic accuracy guarantees of the original Space-Saving algorithm.

Why It Matters

This paper shows that classical streaming summaries can be reformulated to become practical under fully encrypted execution. It sits at the intersection of privacy, databases, and streaming systems, and pushes privacy-preserving analytics closer to real deployment settings.

item.title: Access Paths for Efficient Ordering with Large Language Models
item.description:
This work studies LLM ORDER BY as a semantic database operator and asks a systems question: given an LLM-based ordering task, what physical implementation should a data system choose? The paper evaluates multiple execution strategies for semantic sorting, introduces several algorithmic improvements, and shows that no single access path is uniformly best across datasets and workloads.

Key Ideas

  • Logical vs. physical abstraction: The paper frames LLM ORDER BY as a logical operator whose implementation can vary depending on task characteristics and cost constraints.
  • Improved semantic sorting methods: It strengthens existing semantic sorting algorithms and introduces a semantic-aware external merge sort for LLM-driven ranking.
  • Budget-aware optimization: It proposes an optimizer that combines heuristic rules, LLM-as-judge evaluation, and consensus aggregation to choose a near-optimal access path dynamically.

Main Takeaways

  • Different semantic ordering workloads benefit from different implementations.
  • There is a measurable relationship between compute cost and ranking quality for comparison-based approaches.
  • Careful systems-level optimization can make LLM-powered analytic operators much more practical and robust.

Why It Matters

As LLMs become part of data systems, ranking and sorting by semantics become first-class operations. This paper helps bridge database-style query optimization with LLM-based execution, offering early foundations for principled operator design in large-scale LLM-powered analytics.

item.title: About Me
item.description:
Welcome to my personal space! I am a first-year PhD student at UC Santa Barbara, with a deep love for problem-solving, teaching, and research.

Background

I graduated with a BSc in Computer Science and Engineering from Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology (BUET). I currently work with Professor Divyakant Agrawal and Professor Amr El Abbadi at UCSB. My passion for teaching and mentorship remains a core part of who I am.

item.title: QT-WEAVER
item.description:
Summarizing individual gene trees into species phylogenies using coalescent-based methods has become a standard approach in phylogenomics. However, gene tree estimation error (GTEE)—arising from both analytical factors and biological causes (e.g., short gene sequences)—can adversely impact the accuracy of phylogenomic inference. QT-WEAVER introduces the novel problem of correcting the quartet distribution induced by a set of estimated gene trees.

This involves updating the weights of the quartets to better reflect their relative importance within the gene tree distribution. QT-WEAVER learns conflicts within the quartet distribution induced by a set of gene trees and adjusts the weights to generate an updated quartet distribution.

item.title: WQFM-tree
item.description:
wQFM-TREE is a summary method developed for species tree estimation from multi-locus data, addressing gene tree discordance. It builds on the wQFM method and improves its scalability, making it applicable to large datasets.

Key Features

  • Improved Scalability: Removes the need for explicit quartet generation and weighting, allowing efficient processing of large datasets with many taxa and genes.
  • Polytomy Handling: Unlike wQFM, wQFM-TREE can handle datasets with polytomies.
  • Accuracy: In simulations, wQFM-TREE matches or outperforms ASTRAL in terms of accuracy across various model conditions.
  • Real-World Application: Successfully re-analyzed the green plant dataset from the One Thousand Plant Transcriptomes Initiative, demonstrating its practical utility.

Performance

Simulations show that wQFM-TREE outperforms ASTRAL in 25 out of 27 conditions tested, with significant improvements in 20 of these conditions. It demonstrates reliability and accuracy, even in challenging phylogenetic models involving 200–1000 taxa.

item.title: CHIP-8 Emulator
item.description:
This project is a CHIP-8 emulator written in Golang. The Ebiten game engine was used to create the graphical interface. Developing this project helped me understand how emulators work by simulating the behavior of a physical machine in software.

About CHIP-8

  • CHIP-8 is an old, simple programming language designed for running games in the 1970s.
  • It supports 35 instructions (opcodes), which control tasks like drawing, memory operations, and branching.
  • The system has 16 registers, a 4K memory space, and a stack for handling subroutines.
  • The graphics are black-and-white with a resolution of 64×32 pixels, suitable for basic games.

Project Highlights

  • Implemented Opcodes: Executed all CHIP-8 instructions to run programs correctly.
  • Simulated Hardware: Emulated memory, registers, and the stack for CHIP-8 programs.
  • Graphical Output: Used Ebiten to display the 64×32 monochrome grid.
  • Input Handling: Added support for the CHIP-8 16-key hexadecimal keypad.

Summary

This project introduced me to emulator development by working on a small and simple system. It gave me a clear idea of how instructions, memory, and graphics interact in a machine.

item.title: Yet Another C Compiler
item.description:
This repository contains my implementation of a C Compiler, developed as part of a lab project during my undergraduate studies at BUET. The project encompasses the complete pipeline of compiler construction, progressing through various stages to produce a functional compiler.

Compiler Stages

1. Symbol Table Creation

  • Designed and implemented a hashtable-based symbol table in C++ for efficient management of identifiers.
  • The symbol table supports storing and retrieving variable names, function signatures, and associated attributes.

2. Lexical Analysis

  • Built a lexical analyzer to tokenize the input source code into a sequence of meaningful tokens.
  • Used Flex, a fast and powerful lexical analyzer generator, to define tokenization rules.

3. Parsing

  • Constructed a parser to perform syntactic analysis on the tokenized input, ensuring it adheres to the language grammar.
  • Used Bison, a parser generator, to define a formal context-free grammar for C.
  • Bison constructs a parse tree and identifies syntax errors in the input code during this phase.

4. Intermediate Code Generation

  • Developed the Intermediate Code Generator to produce code compatible with the EMU8086 emulator.
  • This stage translates high-level C constructs into intermediate representations like three-address code (TAC).

About the Project

This C Compiler demonstrates the complete process of compiler construction, from symbol table management to code generation. Originally created as part of the CSE 310 Compiler Lab at BUET.

item.title: xv6 OS Enhancements
item.description:
This project extends the xv6 Operating System, a simple Unix-like OS developed for educational purposes. The enhancements include the implementation of advanced scheduling algorithms, paging, and file system features.

Enhancements and Features

1. Lottery Ticket Scheduling

  • Implemented lottery scheduling, a probabilistic CPU scheduling algorithm.
  • Processes receive “lottery tickets” proportional to their priority; the scheduler randomly selects a ticket to decide which process runs.
  • This approach ensures fair CPU allocation while allowing dynamic adjustment of process priorities.

2. Multi-Level Feedback Queue (MLFQ) Scheduling

  • Added MLFQ scheduling, a priority-based scheduler with multiple queues.
  • Processes dynamically move between queues based on their execution behavior.
  • Balances between responsiveness and throughput, making it suitable for mixed workloads.

3. Paging

  • Implemented paging to support virtual memory management.
  • Used page tables to map virtual addresses to physical memory, enabling efficient memory utilization and protection.

4. File System Enhancements

  • Enhanced the xv6 file system to improve functionality and performance.
  • Added support for advanced features like file metadata handling and optimized disk I/O operations.

About the Project

Originally developed as part of an Operating Systems Lab at BUET, this project highlights proficiency in scheduling algorithms, memory management, and file systems.
